In the text, read in the hemicycle, the deputy appointed by the Government argued that Macau, as the “World Center for Tourism and Leisure” is a region where bookstores “are not mere places to sell books, but also essential cultural spaces in communities, playing a crucial role in promoting the diversification of the tourism sector and sustainable development of the city”. Lam Fat Iam knows what he's talking about. Born in July 1975, he has a PhD in History and presides over the Macau Cultural Education Publications Association, in addition to being director of the Faculty of Human and Social Sciences at the Macau Polytechnic University. In the same intervention, the person responsible suggested that “specific and precise support policies” should be created, namely the creation of “support programs for the exploration and cultural activities of bookstores”, giving “priority to bookstores that promote Macau culture and regularly hold conferences and reading activities”. A system must also be created that encourages “the circulation and publication of local books”, with “priority financing for the planning, editing and publication of books with Macau characteristics”. HM questioned bookstores and publishers in order to understand the gaps in the Macau book market. Anson Ng, from Pin-to Livros & Música, draws attention to the fact that “the market base, in itself, is small, with the book-reading population registering a decrease”. Conversely, “operational costs remain high and there is competition from external online bookstore platforms”. Anson Ng further added that “Macau’s local publishers and local bookstores have not yet managed to form a self-sufficient, healthy and sustainable ecosystem, and that is precisely where supporting policies should focus their efforts”. Regarding the position of deputy Lam Fat Iam, the head of Pin-to Livros & Música says that his ideas follow “an acceptable direction”, however, “an in-depth discussion” is necessary regarding “details of implementation” of the ideas expressed in the interpellation. “Before establishing concrete support policies, we hope that relevant government departments can carefully study the experiences of other regions”, in addition to maintaining “extensive communication with the current industry in Macau”. For Anson Ng, we must also try to “truly understand the needs of the sector”, as “only in this way will it be possible to develop transparent, fair and practical support policies”. Contact difficulties Catarina Mesquita, founder of Mandarina, which publishes children's books, does not have a physical store, but sells her books in spaces such as Livraria Portuguesa. In response to HM, Catarina Mesquita suggests “that the Government should, together with schools, be able to place orders with bookstores and directly with publishers”, speaking of the specific case of “Na Rua”, one of the Mandarina editions that she tried to place directly in schools. "The book 'Na Rua' talks about the history of Macau and I have tried, many times, for this book to be introduced in schools, not only because it has a history of the place where these children live, but also because it has many aspects of the history of Macau, and very specific characteristics [of the territory], which is talked about so much. And I have tried several times to contact the education services [Directorate of Education and Youth Development Services], I was unable to get that contact and it is very difficult. I have had people from the Government in presentation sessions about the Mandarin, but I didn’t get any feedback”, he laments. Catarina Mesquita therefore argues that the Government should “together with schools, place orders with bookstores and directly with publishers”. "You end up falling into a hole that you don't really know what it is. This effort should be more valued, not in the sense of being praised, but in the sense of taking Macau's works and taking them into classrooms, which is the most efficient and effective way of getting them to children, in the case of children's literature. I think it is not something that is very difficult to do", he assumed. The subsidy issue It is true that the Executive has presented some measures to promote reading, and Lam Fat Iam noted this in his plenary intervention. “In recent years, the Macao SAR Government and society have already achieved results in promoting reading and building a ‘Reading City'”, he described, particularly on the part of the Cultural Institute, with the creation of events such as the “Reading Festival”, “Library Week”, “Month of Joint Reading throughout the City of Macau” and the “Reading Program for Babies and Children”. There is, in his view, a “solid basis for a social environment favorable to reading”, but “physical bookstores, as essential spaces for reading, face survival difficulties”, highlighted the deputy. Lam Fat Iam also highlighted “the limited size of the market, high rental costs and a fragile chain of production and sales of local publications created an environment with ‘difficulty in publishing, buying and selling books'”. Something that, in the deputy's opinion, “makes it difficult for many valuable Macau books to reach the hands of residents and visitors”. Catarina Mesquita also draws attention to the fact that Macau has many published books and is “a very rich territory” in terms of its history and culture, but that more needs to be done beyond the simple subsidy to publish a book. In the case of Mandarina, the first five books were made “without any subsidies or support”. "Perhaps due to a personality trait, I didn't want Mandarina, in the beginning, to be the result of support or subsidies. I wanted what I achieved to be done by me, and that made me work much harder", he assures. Catarina Mesquita therefore feels that the financial support, or this initial subsidy, “is enough to produce a book, and that’s it”, when, in fact, “this support is more important later in terms of distribution, ordering, people being able to have the book in their hands”. And he argues that more should be done to support the distribution of works. “There is no support, or bridge” in this sense, he considers. "There is so much talk about the platform with Portuguese-speaking countries, but there is no support for the distribution and transport of books to regions other than Macau, and this is fundamental. I have come across this problem, because at the moment Mandarina is starting its first steps in Portugal and I have no way of getting the books to the country without having any type of support." In the responsible's view, the connection between Portugal and the territory could be explored more effectively. "In terms of laws and taxes we pay on imports, a partnership that would make us benefit from it made sense. Therefore, I think there is a hole here that needs to be resolved quickly." The four measures Lam Fat Iam also left, in his intervention, suggestions to improve the distribution system for editorial works, defending the construction of a “virtuous system for the circulation and publication of local books”, with “priority financing for the planning, editing and publication of books with Macau characteristics”. Regarding “public services, public libraries, community libraries and primary and secondary schools financed by the Government, must place orders for books in local bookstores”. Furthermore, in connection with urban renewal projects, the deputy believes that the “Bookstore +” model can be developed, defining “guidelines” and reserving “cultural spaces to encourage the integration of bookstores with cafes, art galleries, cultural and creative stores and even revitalization projects for historic buildings”. For the deputy, bookstores must be “multifunctional cultural landmarks that integrate reading, leisure, exhibitions and cultural exchange”, and there must also be support for participation in local and regional book fairs. HM contacted other bookstores in Macau, but at the time of writing this edition had received no further responses.

item.title: Rota das Letras | Film by Filipa Queiroz tells the story of homeless people from Alta de Coimbra
item.description: “Salatinas – Stories from the Old Alta of Coimbra” brings journalist Filipa Queiroz back to Macau, together with Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira, co-authors of the documentary that is part of the Rota das Letras poster. The film tells the story of how, during the Estado Novo, around three thousand residents were expelled from Alta de Coimbra to allow the construction of the University City The University City in Alta de Coimbra is one of the city's tourist attractions, offering a stunning landscape. However, despite the beauty, the history of the construction of Cidade Universitária is full of suffering. In the documentary “Salatinas – Histórias da Velha Alta de Coimbra”, Filipa Queiroz, Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira tell the story of the people who lived there in the 1940s and who found themselves suddenly homeless and without a plan B. The film is part of the poster for the Rota das Letras Literary Festival, which starts on March 5th. “We hope that, in Macau, the story can have an impact on everyone and in particular on the many people from Coimbra who we know reside in the territory”, Filipa Queiroz, who lived in Macau for several years, told HM and TDM – Macau Television. He is happy to return and to reveal this story unknown to many. And he gives the greatest praise to a festival that saw its birth 15 years ago. "I find the programming of this edition of Rota das Letras particularly courageous and relevant taking into account the contexts of Portugal, Macau and the world at the moment. We try to present the film where it makes sense and this is precisely the case. It is a film about people, but also about power, resistance, identity and the price of progress – themes that Macau knows so well", he highlighted. Filipa Queiroz came into contact with the story of these people through a report. “Rafael Vieira collaborated with me as a journalist, as he ran a publication in Coimbra [Coimbra Coolectiva] and told me that there was an [interesting] story there, and that he wanted to tell stories outside the box, more invisible, so to speak.” “Salatinas” was born, in reference to the name given to the people who lived in Alta de Coimbra, where there were already faculties of Literature, Law and the library. However, the Estado Novo evicted these people to build the currently existing buildings. Three thousand people left their homes, making this “one of the biggest urban transformations in Portugal, at a time when forced evictions, lack of access to housing and social segregation still make headlines”, describes the documentary’s synopsis. “Salatinas” is, therefore, “a trip down memory lane of the historic neighborhood wiped off the map under the regime’s impetus for renewal and the hubbub of students, tricanas, grocers and tailors that hides beneath the colleges”, adds the same synopsis. Leave quickly “What happened was the will of the Government at that time”, says Filipa Queiroz, to explain how a political decision changed the lives of so many people. "The University of Coimbra moved between Lisbon and Coimbra for many years, but then it was located in Coimbra and was the only one in the country, where the country's brains, the elite, were. There was then a decision that the university needed to grow and it had to be there. Salazar himself had studied there [Faculty of Law], in addition to there being a hospital. It wasn't about requalification, but they really destroyed what there was to build on top." What followed was a profound transformation in families in the area. Many never fully recovered. "When the destruction began, basically the Government warned the people who lived there that they all had to get out of there. They gave values ​​to the houses [for compensation], completely disproportionate and much lower than the value of the houses." "There is a person we mention in the film who was part of Salazar's circle, she was a doctor and an illustrious person, and she managed to have her house be one of the last to be removed, but she was still [homeless]. The poorer and more humble the people were, the quicker they had to leave, with one hand in front, the other behind. These people were thrown into neighborhoods of the city, which we can't even call neighborhoods, because it was something improvised at the time", says the co-author of the documentary. We are talking about areas with “fragile” houses. However, the precariousness of housing already came from behind. "Little houses were built that weren't of incredible quality, but the houses in Alta de Coimbra weren't there either, many of them. There was no basic sanitation, that needed to be requalified." A “self-identity” Filipa Queiroz highlights how “the solution was to end everything, not valuing the fact that it is a neighborhood, and with its own identity, and this is the main issue”. The neighborhood was Salatina. "Salatinas are people who worked or lived in the upper part of the city, or who were born there, and who crossed paths daily with the student elite. This diversity was very rich, which ended up having effects on the cultural aspect, on music, for example." Coimbra is a land associated with names in Portuguese music, especially interventionists, such as Zeca Afonso and Carlos Paredes, and among the “doctors” there were also “the futricas”, that is, “the people of Coimbra, but no one ever remembered those people”. Filipa Queiroz highlights that “many people, including those from Coimbra, don’t know what happened there”. “There was a city there, a very concentrated urban network full of people and life, with São João bonfires, cultural events, all very identity-related things.” All things destroyed with the requalification and expansion of the University City. Filipa Queiroz highlights that nowadays, and despite university experiences, the city's Alta no longer has the dynamics it once had. “It has that austere architecture and at night, nothing happens.” “Salatinas” gave voice to a topic that is rarely talked about. "People are very moved and feel very valued. We had people who went to the premiere [in Coimbra, in the Alta area] and who told us: 'It seems that there has been a repair here'. This even led to the death of some people, because they committed suicide out of grief, because they had to leave the place where they built their business and their lives". Filipa Queiroz also thanks Ricardo Pinto, director of Rota das Letras, for the invitation made. "He invited us knowing only the idea of ​​the film. This will allow my colleagues to discover, for the first time, this place, Macau, which is so special to me." Filipa Queiroz wants to continue telling stories through images. Having made documentaries linked to Macau, such as “Boat People” and “Once Upon a Time in Ka-Hó”, the idea is to continue in an audiovisual genre so close to journalism. "Documentaryism adds an artistic layer and time that journalism does not allow. Yes, I would like to make [more documentaries], that is always on my horizon. There are always challenges, with financing the work and the time that can be dedicated to it, but without a doubt, yes, I would like to continue."

item.title: Heritage | Stories of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro told in a book
item.description: Natalie Fu has just released a book about traditional commerce on Rua Cinco de Outubro, at the invitation of the Macau Cultural Heritage Reinvention Studies Association. “Macao’s Historical House Taxonomy – The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de Outubro” tells the centuries-old story of shophouses that were the “heart of Macau’s Chinese community” A book has just been published that reveals a little about the history of commerce carried out by the Chinese community on Rua Cinco de Outubro, located in Porto Interior, the scene of many commercial movements over the centuries. The work also explains how these stores, which are at the same time houses, reveal details about the history of Macau. “Macao’s Historical House Taxonomy – The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de Outubro”, written by Natalie Fu, a doctoral student at the University of São José, was invited by the Association for Studies on the Reinvention of Cultural Heritage of Macau, whose edition also depends on “the support and assistance of many other people”, the author told HM. One of the book's sales outlets, which does not have a Portuguese version, is Pin-to Livros. “The investigation began with the love for Macau and its old buildings”, reveals the author, who began the research work around these buildings in 2022, in the middle of the pandemic. "Rua de Cinco de Outubro has always been an important commercial area in Macau. Once, it was the heart of Macau's Chinese community. Even today, many old stores remain close to the local neighborhood. The stores are mixed-use buildings", explains the author. This is the stage for commercial diversity, as Rua Cinco de Outubro “is home to several businesses, such as traditional Chinese bakeries, Chinese medicine stores, tea shops and fruit shops”. According to the presentation of the work, made on social media by the association itself, this type of property constitutes “a common type of multifunctional residential building”, where the “plan of the buildings is generally rectangular, with two to three floors”. Typically, the ground floor is used for the store or as a warehouse, while the upper floors are reserved for housing. "This type of construction can adapt to the local climate, presenting great flexibility in the construction methods and materials used. Due to the diversity of daily uses, shop houses were once the main type of building on the streets of Macau, having important historical, social, cultural, architectural and aesthetic value", it further reads. Preservation Challenges Still according to the same note, the book draws attention to the importance of preserving this type of construction and, consequently, a symbol of local heritage. “Unfortunately, because they are small-scale and scattered buildings, and because they are not fully protected by the Macau Heritage Protection Law, they have been gradually disappearing with the rapid development of society”, and, currently, “only a small number of these buildings have the classification of ‘monument’ and are protected”. For Natalie Fu, “the preservation of Macau's stores faces many challenges, one of which is the fact that they are not included in the existing list of protected old buildings in Macau”, in addition to that, in her view, “changes in construction laws have also made it difficult to maintain these old buildings”. Asked about the future of this iconic street in Porto Interior, the author recognizes that “the growth of a city inevitably involves the renovation and disappearance of buildings”, so “determining which buildings should be preserved and how to preserve them is a long-term challenge”. Natalie Fu even talks about the need to extend the investigation to more streets in Macau that are also traditional shopping areas. "Research and documentation of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro and in Macau are urgent as their number is dwindling. Additionally, additional academic investigations and discussions with the local community are needed to inform the future direction of the Inner Harbor area." In relation to the panorama on Rua Cinco de Outubro, the author describes that maintenance should be focused on, keeping commercial spaces open. "Stores that are kept in use better highlight their social value. Taking some stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro as an example, the existence of century-old stores further emphasizes the cultural and historical value of the area and the stores. Furthermore, 'keeping them in use' allows for more immediate monitoring and maintenance, which is the most direct way of protecting ancient architecture." A certain influence In a territory characterized by the coexistence of Portuguese and Chinese cultures, Natalie Fu's book shows how these houses were influenced by other cultures. “Chinese and Western architecture in Macau have always influenced each other throughout historical development, and this feature is particularly visible in the appearance of the shop houses.” In this way, this book helps to cement this characteristic, “establishing a classification system for Macau’s shop houses, consolidating their architectural definition”. This book is, therefore, a “classifying study of the old shop houses in Macau”, constituting “the first step towards their safeguarding”, describes, in the same note, the association that supported the editorial and research project. "The author carried out a detailed record and survey [of the stores], starting with visits and cartography surveys in the Porto Interior area. Maps were drawn up of the location of the shop houses on that street, analysis sheets based on fieldwork, photographic records and interviews with current traders and residents, in order to understand the history of the use of each building." Furthermore, the work documents seven cases in particular, “providing information on their history, evolution and current use, as well as a preliminary discussion on possible preservation directions”. The book also records two examples of shop houses that have now disappeared. “It is hoped that this work will encourage more people to discover the ‘beauty of Macau.’ With the shop houses as a central theme, it is expected to promote exchange and discussion between academics from different areas”, the association also points out. Natalie Fu invited “several experts and academics to share their ideas, experiences and suggestions on the protection and future of this type of buildings”, it is described. Born and raised in Macau, Natalie Fu had the opportunity to visit all of Southeast Asia from a young age, trips that led her to “foster a deep appreciation and passion for architecture”. The author has a degree in Architecture, also describing herself as a person interested in the area of ​​heritage conservation and a “historical architecture scholar trained in Macau”.

item.title: Diaspora | Greater connection between Casa de Macau and Casa de Goa defended
item.description: The conference “Casa de Goa and Casa de Macau: fundamental agents of an indispensable dialogue” took place in Lisbon yesterday, which analyzed the connecting role that the entities can have in the Goan and Macau diaspora. Carlos Piteira, president of Casa de Macau, highlights the importance of creating bridges with Casa de Goa through the “parallel representation of Portuguese descendants” What role can Casa de Macau and Casa de Goa, in Lisbon, have, taking into account the passage of time and the developments brought by the new generations of the Macanese and Goan communities? It is true that Macau and Goa have long ceased to be part of the former Portuguese empire, but sociocultural ties remain. With this in mind, the lecture “Casa de Goa and Casa de Macau: fundamental agents of an indispensable dialogue” took place yesterday at the Lisbon Geography Society, which sought to outline answers to the challenges of the future. To HM, Carlos Piteira, president of the Casa de Macau in Lisbon, declared that “it would be interesting if the bridges between the Casa de Macau and the Casa de Goa could also be considered in parallel with their representation of Portuguese descendants and mixed cultures”, taking into account that the two entities “are fundamental pillars for the representation of these communities”. The person responsible, himself Macanese, highlights “the similarities of their gastronomy, Creole and festivities” between the two communities. It would therefore be “interesting to combine the small representations of the giants of the Asian world, represented by the symbolism of the Dragon and the Elephant, as structuring elements for a dialogue between Portugal and Asia, political and institutional wills permit”. In Carlos Piteira's view, the two houses “reinforce the legitimized heritage of the dialogue between the West and the East, and Portugal's centuries-old relations with these countries, often ignored and forgotten by official and institutional powers”. Pedro Colaço, from the management of Casa de Goa, highlights the importance of preserving the memory of the Portuguese presence both in Goa and in the remaining territories of Daman and Diu. "There have been 450 years of history, in the case of Macau it was a few more decades, and we try to keep up with current Goan culture. One of the most important parts of our work is to build a bridge between Goa and Lisbon, and on a daily basis we contact artists, musicians and members of the community, that is our role", he described to HM. Pedro Colaço gave the example of the concert that will take place on March 8th at Fundação Oriente, in Lisbon, entitled “Oscar Castellino – Voz de Ópera de Goa and Piano with Rodrigo Ayala”. Furthermore, Casa de Goa, currently without its own headquarters, seeks, like Casa de Macau, to attract new members, especially from the new generations, in order to continue the project, so that the Casa remains “as a hub of unity for Goans and, increasingly, for friends of Goa”. Follow the story Casa de Macau was founded in Lisbon in 1966 and has followed different phases not only in the history of Portugal but also in the country's relationship with Macau and China. "The existence of the House of Macau undoubtedly marks a parallel with the history of Portugal itself for more than 70 years. Macau was the 'pearl of the East' for the Estado Novo and lived through the turmoil of the revolutionary period of April 1974, adapting and establishing itself as the 'bridge between the West and East'". Finally, after the transition to the People's Republic of China, we have seen the reorganization [of Macau] as a central territory in the connection, from China, with Portuguese-speaking countries.” Carlos Piteira also described that the House of Macau has followed “the history of the times, expanding the vision of the Portuguese presence in this territory”. Furthermore, he had a “no less important” task, having “crystallized, protected and perpetuated the Macanese community in Portugal and the world”. Currently, Casa de Macau has developed several activities that bring more members to Avenida Gago Coutinho, where it is located, and whose building has recently undergone restoration work. Every Wednesday there are Macanese food lunches with Chef Tina, originally from Macau, as well as film sessions and lectures. The Casa's mission is currently “to carry out activities to promote Macau and Macanese culture”, providing “support and promotion of studies and works of scientific content about Macau, Macau people” and also providing “institutional interconnection with other entities linked to Macau, Portugal and Casas de Macau in the diaspora”. Who is from Goa and who is not Pedro Colaço explained that the Goan community in Lisbon “has an easier time talking to the remaining Indian diaspora” in Portugal, taking into account that, for example, “the connection is very old” with the Hindu community. “We are sorry that sometimes [the community] is not talked about more in the media, because it is an important part of Portuguese history”, laments the person in charge, remembering prominent figures in the country, such as António Costa, who was Portuguese prime minister, or Narana Coissoró, former president of Casa de Goa and former deputy of the CDS-PP. “There are more notorious figures, but in general the Goan community has integrated itself very well and is known for being a very educated community”, he also recalled, regretting that in Portugal it is not possible to make a distinction between communities of Indian origin. "One thing that is worrying is the lack of knowledge that exists in relation to the communities. Nowadays there is a lot of pressure due to immigration and then everything gets mixed up", highlights Pedro Colaço, remembering that there is a strong feeling in being Goan, particularly in connection with Catholicism and a very unique culture as opposed to Hindustani communities that are currently very present in Portugal. "The Goan community is so mixed that half of my family, or more, do not appear to be Goan, but I, by chance, do. There is a feeling of being Goan, something that unites us. The other communities of the Indian diaspora are often confessional, they have an associated religion, while the Goan community is, for the most part, Catholic, but everyone goes to their own church, it is very spread out", he pointed out. The future is a challenge Pedro Colaço speaks of a “challenging future” for Casa de Goa. "I'm now in my second term, the leadership has changed president, and one of the things we've been trying to do is to bring young people into the governing bodies. We now have three or four younger members, under the age of 40. We've also been trying to expand the House to non-Goans." In this point, there are similarities with the Casa de Macau, which has also sought to expand the range of members to Chinese or Portuguese people who do not belong to the community. Pedro Colaço adds that, over the years, things have changed. "30 years ago the community was very large, but at this moment, and like all institutions, we have to expand. There are many people who really like Goa and who know a lot about the territory, perhaps even more than me, and they are the ones who have to be pulled", he assumed. Currently, Casa de Goa has 500 members and, even without a headquarters, it seeks to carry out activities, particularly in partnership with Fundação Oriente. A monthly bulletin is published. They no longer have headquarters due to the metro works in Lisbon, which has made the House's agenda difficult. “We are in the process of finding a new headquarters, and it is something that has been a constraint, but we try to carry out as many activities as possible and, every month, we always have activities”, concluded Pedro Colaço.

item.title: Prison | Guard accepted bribes to let goods in
item.description:
The main target of the CCAC investigation is said to have received “at least” 17 thousand patacas to let products into the prison, which were then sold by an inmate, who profited from the scheme
The Commissioner against Corruption (CCAC) announced that it had discovered the case of a prison guard from the Directorate of Correctional Services (DSC) who accepted bribes to help an inmate “smuggle products into the prison establishment”. The investigation also focused on another prison guard, who is currently retired. “Following the investigation, the CCAC verified that the active prison guard had taken advantage of his duties to help an inmate smuggle products into the prison area”, it was announced. When the goods reached the inmate, he would sell them inside the prison to profit from illegality. “The inmate in question paid, through his wife, to the prison guard an amount of at least 17 thousand patacas in return for his help”, it was explained. This prison guard is suspected of committing the crimes of passive corruption for an illicit act, which carries a penalty of up to eight years in prison, and the crime of document forgery, which carries a penalty of up to three years in prison. The investigation did not stop at bribery or contraband inside the prison. The authorities also announced that the prison guard “had already committed speeding offenses twice”. However, it was discovered that the guard, “to avoid responsibility”, “had resorted to a family member and a friend to take the blame”. The family members and friend, who according to the investigation were used as scapegoats, are suspected of committing the crime of document forgery, the penalty for which can be up to three years in prison.
Other involved In relation to the second prison guard investigated, who is now retired, it was indicated that “he provided assistance, during the time he was carrying out his duties, to another inmate in smuggling products into the prison establishment”. This prison guard was also investigated for having “helped” the inmate “to exchange messages, secretly and on several occasions, with his family”. In view of the investigation, the retiree is suspected of committing the crime of abuse of power, punishable by a prison sentence of up to three years. According to the CCAC, the cases were forwarded to the Public Ministry and communicated to the DSC. Reacting to the investigation, the DSC stated that it was “deeply saddened by the fact that some prison guards have knowingly broken the law and violated their duties, seriously damaging the reputation of the prison police”. DSC further indicated that “it takes this matter very seriously and will fully cooperate with the judicial authorities in the criminal investigation”. “DSC reiterates that it has zero tolerance for any illegal or disciplinary breaches by any member of staff and will deal with them seriously, in accordance with the law, in order to uphold the rule of law and maintain the reputation and credibility of the correctional service,” it added.

item.title: Shuttle Bus | Government says it asked operators for more services
item.description:
After the controversy over the lack of alternatives to ensure condominium shuttle services, the Government defends itself and indicates that it has asked public bus operators to provide more vehicles
Regarding the difficulties faced by residential buildings in providing shuttle services, the Government indicates that it has asked public transport operators to provide more buses. The explanations appear in a response to an interpellation by deputy Chan Hao Weng, which is signed by Chan U Tong, director of the Services for Labor Affairs (DSAL). The difficulties in contracting bus services by condominiums in large housing complexes became a concern for deputies, with the entry into force of the law on the activity of travel agencies and the profession of tourist guide, which prohibits travel agencies from providing this type of transport service. Now the Government says that it has asked public bus operators to make more buses available. “The Macao SAR Government, through interdepartmental coordination, promoted the rental of their ‘non-operational vehicles’ to the two bus operators to provide transport services, with a view to meeting the travel needs of residents of the housing complexes in question”, it was stated. At the same time, the Government argued that it requested more buses to run near the buildings: “DSAT also asked bus operators to increase attention to the waiting situation at stops around the complexes, increasing the frequency of services in a timely manner to better transport the flow of passengers”, it was added. Despite the response, in which the director of DSAL cites DSAT's position, the issue of increasing transport costs is not addressed by government officials. Previous ban As for the ban on travel agency buses being hired for this shuttle service, the Government insists that the measure was in force even before the new law, despite the widespread practice. “Between 2015 and 2025, there were more than 140 procedures initiated against travel agencies that violated the rules regarding the use of vehicles, including the illegal provision of tourist buses as buses for workers or as buses connecting to housing complexes”, it was indicated. The numbers presented represent an average of 12.7 “procedures initiated” per year. “As a supervisory entity, DST has supervised, under the terms of the law, the activity of travel agencies, and as long as the illegal provision of services by travel agencies is verified, the respective procedure will be initiated”, it was stressed.

item.title: Sex and love at the end of the world
item.description: One of the things that gives me the most pleasure in writing about sex is that it transports us to a universe of curiosity and socio-cultural connection. It's a serious and fun activity, even if it's just to see that frowning face still blush and become uncomfortable because there's mention of vulvas and penises. Sex has this quality: its secrecy makes its exposure seductive. It's a territory full of mines, but we can only avoid them if we jump around. But the world seems darker with each passing day, and I don't even know how to connect with this lightness. I have written here several times about current events that seemed relevant to me to discuss the topic of sexuality. About the state of human rights, about sex as a weapon of violence, about misogyny and misinformation as a plague that affronts us. About teenage boys who have the desire to kill girls, about people who are raped and abused in Gaza, about women and men who resist wars in this world, about women who cannot access a safe abortion, even when their lives depend on it. About homophobia, biphobia and transphobia that is alive. We live in such strange times that it's hard to think of sex as something fun. I don't even know if we have the right to have fun when we seem to be on the brink of a third world war. Or maybe this is the right time. Perhaps the best form of resistance is to think about pleasure and connection when everything falls apart before our eyes. I choose to still think about sex because maybe Freud was right. The sexual drive is the life drive – confusing and disorganizing, but with vitality and desire. And in the face of catastrophe, it is in sex that I see a safe haven. Physiologically, the sexual act transports you to that place of deep intimacy and connection. Intimate contact activates neurochemical systems associated with security and attachment. The release of oxytocin, endorphins, and dopamine during intimacy isn't just about pleasure; It is also a form of emotional regulation. In a world saturated with threats, the human body seeks experiences that restore a sense of security. It is also in sex – and in love – that I can see an identity and political purpose in the chaos. Sex may be contested and criticized in reprehensible ways, but it is in sex that there is also the community, that thing bigger than the genitals themselves. When bell hooks spoke of love, he spoke of this place that I consider sex to be found. A place that is not guided by emotion and attraction alone, but by choices that are ethically informed. For the author, love is not an individual experience, but a radical act of social ethics capable of transforming relationships and society. Audre Lorde also described the erotic as a form of power — not just sexual, but existential. For her, the erotic is what reminds us that we are alive and that pleasure does not need to ask the world for permission to exist. Perhaps this is why authoritarian regimes have always tried to control sexuality: because bodies that feel pleasure are also bodies that demand autonomy. So, if I seem frivolous to you because the things of love and sex have always seemed secondary in the grand scheme of life, it's because you don't understand my priorities. I am fed up with the conflict and suffering in the world. I'm thirsty for good news, for development that goes against the state of things. But knowing that I have to live in the place that I don't want to ignore, I also take refuge in this possibility of writing about sex – as I said, in a fun and serious way – as a political act of care. Talking about sex when the world seems to be falling apart is not necessarily a form of distraction. It could be exactly the opposite: perhaps it's a way of remembering that, while there are bodies that seek intimacy, we haven't completely given up on each other.

item.title: New Neighborhood | Request for greater circulation of domestic workers
item.description:
Representative Wong Kit Cheng is in Beijing to participate in the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), where she asked that domestic workers hired in Macau can work throughout the Cooperation Zone, and not just in the New Neighborhood of Macau
Wong Kit Cheng, a deputy linked to the Macau Women's Association, defended the facilitation of the policy that allows local residents to take foreign domestic workers to the Deep Cooperation Zone on Mountain Island. The position was taken by Wong in Beijing, where he is participating in the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), as a representative of the Macao SAR. According to her intervention, which was shared through the Women's Association portal, Wong began by highlighting the importance of implementing this policy, limited to the New Neighborhood of Macau, and indicated that this positive result was only possible due to the governors of the Interior. However, the legislator suggested that the process become more comprehensive, as there are currently only 62 non-resident workers working as domestic servants in this neighborhood of Hengqin, where only Macau residents can live. Based on official data, Wong indicated that only 14 percent of residents across the entire Deep Cooperation Zone use non-resident workers hired in Macau. However, studies on the subject, mentioned by the deputy, indicate that more than half of Macau residents in Hengqin intend to use non-resident workers hired in Macau, instead of labor available in the interior.
Next level Based on the scenario described, Wong Kit Cheng indicated that greater use of non-resident workers hired in Macau in the Interior can be achieved through two changes to current policies. The first way is to facilitate circulation in the Deep Cooperation Zone with domestic workers hired in Macau being able to move throughout the Zone, instead of being limited to the New Neighborhood of Macau. According to Wong, this way Macau residents living and working on the other side of the border can better experience the benefits of national policies. Secondly, the deputy linked to the Women's Association admits the creation of a new security system between Macau and Guangdong, to control the entry and movement of these employees. According to Wong Kit Cheng, this new mechanism would allow residents to use this workforce, while mitigating security risks in the Interior. The deputy also highlighted that a mechanism of this type must also guarantee the labor protection of these workers.

item.title: From wife to porn actress: Opening of traditional marriage (I)
item.description: According to the Japanese press, a woman in her thirties suffered respiratory arrest due to problems related to anesthesia, after undergoing surgery for a fracture. This near-death experience led her to reflect on life. Due to the prolonged absence of marital intimacy, she felt emotionally and physically deprived. So she decided to speak openly with her husband, suggesting that she start having extramarital affairs, which shocked and disturbed him. After several conversations, the couple agreed to maintain a traditional marriage relationship, maintaining their daily routine and sharing household tasks. However, the change would consist in the fact that women began to act in films for adults, which showed their beautiful and voluptuous body; he would even appreciate and comment on your acting talent. With this change, the woman's physical and emotional needs would be met by other people. Thus, the relationship went from a “traditional” marriage to an “open” marriage. An “open marriage” is a transparent and honest relationship that involves mutual consent, unlike traditional marriage. In traditional marriage, infidelity implies betrayal and lying; however, in an open marriage, relationships with other people are the result of mutual agreement, and there is no forced acceptance or hiding. The couple establishes detailed rules. For example: is openness limited to socializing or does it include sexual activity? Can the outer element be a common friend? Can you take your new partner home? Are contraceptive and preventive measures necessary? In an open marriage, how is the emotional superiority of the spouse over the new partner established? The answer is simple: when one of the couple needs the other, the “other” has to leave immediately. This is the most important principle to maintain the core of the relationship. Why do you choose an open marriage? Satisfying physiological needs is one of the reasons. In the story mentioned above, one of the couple had more sexual desire than the other; open marriage could thus serve as a compensatory mechanism to maintain marital stability. Secondly, the husband agreeing to his wife's participation in adult films allowed her to have new sexual experiences and new emotional connections and increase her self-knowledge. Third, rather than repressing nature or preventing infidelity, conditional openness allows for a more lasting trust because there is no need for anyone to hide. Although open marriage has advantages and appears to be liberating, it also deals with the following difficulties: First, few people can remain truly indifferent when confronted with their spouse's intimacy with others, or their appreciation for them. Jealousy can turn consent into opposition, and this opposition can take violent forms. Second, society generally views open marriage as an abnormal relationship, which can potentially damage marital happiness and leave an impression of “promiscuity.” From a legal perspective, it can be considered a violation of marital fidelity and a challenge to social norms. Next week, we will continue our analysis of the impact on Japanese society of traditional marriages transitioning to open marriages.

item.title: MP | Assumed role of interlocution between China and PLP
item.description:
Tong Hio Fong promises that Macau will play the role of jurisdictional interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries
The Attorney General of Macau, Tong Hio Fong, pointed out that the city's Public Prosecutor's Office (MP) will play the role of jurisdictional interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries. During the Two Sessions taking place in Beijing, the Central Government of China once again highlighted Macau's strategic role in connecting with Portuguese-speaking countries. The work report presented by Prime Minister Li Qiang highlighted that the region must enhance its unique advantages and deepen its role as an interconnection platform between the interior of China and the Portuguese-speaking space, in order to promote “long-term prosperity and stability”. As the Public Ministry has now highlighted, in a statement reacting to the Two Sessions, the Macao SAR will be consolidated as a platform for judicial cooperation and professional communication, in order to bring legal systems closer together and promote exchanges between magistrates and institutions in Portuguese-speaking countries. The same organization intends to deepen collaboration in the Guangdong–Hong Kong–Macau Greater Bay Area and in the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin (Mountain Island), but always with the perspective that Macau should serve as a “bridgehead” for China's opening to the Portuguese-speaking world. The Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay is a project by Beijing to integrate the two territories of Hong Kong, Macau and nine cities in Guangdong province in a region with more than 86 million inhabitants and an economy of more than one billion euros in 2023. Defined objective According to Tong, the objective is for Macau to increasingly assert itself as a “privileged link between China and the Portuguese-speaking international community”, contributing to national development and consolidating its strategic position within the ‘One Country, Two Systems’ policy. Macau's government plans for 2026 include the signing of an agreement for mutual legal assistance in criminal matters with Angola. Angola and mainland China signed an extradition treaty in 2006, which was only approved by the Angolan Parliament in 2011, and was ratified by the Government of Luanda two years later. According to the Lines of Government Action for next year, Macau also wants to start negotiating an “agreement on the reciprocal confirmation and execution of judicial decisions in civil and commercial matters” with Portugal. However, the document does not mention any date for the conclusion of negotiations and the eventual signing of an agreement after all. Macau and Portugal signed an agreement in 2019 regarding the surrender of fugitive offenders, whose criminal legality was called into question by the Portuguese Bar Association. The protocol is not in force, as it was not voted on in the Assembly of the Republic.

item.title: Hong Kong | 10 million stolen from Macau resident and family member
item.description: A Macau resident and a family member from Hong Kong were the target of a robbery worth around 10 million Hong Kong dollars on Friday at Sheung Wan metro station. The case was revealed by the Hong Kong police, and the thieves were arrested on the spot. According to information released at a press conference, the victims were robbed by two thieves, after being threatened with a knife. The robbery happened shortly after the Macau resident and his family member exchanged currency at a currency exchange store on Hillier Street. In this operation, the victims exchanged US dollars and renminbi for 10 million Hong Kong dollars. After the robbery, the thieves were detained, because agents quickly appeared at the scene. A police officer also intercepted a car that was nearby and detained the driver, who is believed to be an accomplice of the detainees. The police superintendent, Lo Ka Chun, revealed that during the robbery and arrest, only the Macau resident was injured, slightly, in one hand. The injuries occurred during initial resistance to the robbery, and although they were not serious, the Macau resident was taken to Queen Mary Hospital. Lo Ka Chun also acknowledged that the police had already received a tip about the possibility that a criminal association was planning this robbery. According to the initial investigation, the three detainees hold Hong Kong resident identity cards and one of them has a Philippine passport. Furthermore, some detainees have ties to the triads. This is the third case of its kind in the last three months in Sheung Wan.

item.title: The Geopolitics of Flashpoint
item.description: (Continued from March 5th edition) At the same time, Israel faced internal and external pressure to act. The possibility of a nuclear Iran was seen as an intolerable threat, and sections of the defense establishment argued that inaction could result in an irreversible shift in the regional balance of power. The United States, although reluctant to become involved in a new conflict in the Middle East, was bound by security commitments that made it difficult to remain on the sidelines if Israel was the target of significant attacks. Thus, on February 28th, the regional system found itself in a situation of critical instability. Deterrence was weakened, diplomatic channels were blocked and the margin for miscalculation was minimal. From this point, any incident such as a proxy attack, misinterpretation, or miscalculated military move could trigger a rapid and potentially devastating escalation. After February 28, the Middle East entered a new phase of uncertainty following the death of Iranian supreme leader Ali Khamenei in a joint attack by the United States and Israel. In the early hours of that day, a wave of large-scale aerial bombings hit military and government targets in Tehran and other strategic areas of the country. Among the targets was the complex where Khamenei worked and lived; It was there that, according to later confirmation from the White House and Israeli officials, the 86-year-old leader was killed, along with senior officials from the Revolutionary Guards and close members of his family. For a few hours, official Iranian silence fueled rumors and counterinformation. Formal confirmation came via Iranian state media, which announced that the “Leader of the Islamic Revolution” had been “martyred” in the attack by “America and the Zionist regime” on the morning of Saturday, February 28. A 40-day national mourning was declared, accompanied by several public holidays, while the Iranian Constitution provided that a council composed of the President, the head of the judiciary and a member of the Council of Guardians would provisionally assume leadership roles in the absence of a designated successor. The news of Khamenei's death triggered deeply contrasting reactions inside and outside Iran. In several Iranian cities, especially in Tehran, there were demonstrations of mourning, with people dressed in black gathering in central squares, crying and chanting religious and political slogans. At the same time, in other urban areas, especially in neighborhoods with a history of contesting the regime, horns, loud music and even fireworks were heard, in a spontaneous celebration of the end of a leader associated with decades of repression. The Iranian diaspora, spread across Western capitals, also reacted intensely. In London, Frankfurt, Toronto and other cities, thousands of Iranians and supporters joined in demonstrations that mixed relief, euphoria and a sense of the end of an era. Videos shared on social media showed crowds dancing, shouting anti-regime slogans and celebrating what many described as the first opportunity in decades to imagine a different political future for Iran. In parallel, the Iranian state apparatus sought to project an image of continuity and resistance. The military and the Revolutionary Guards launched missiles and drones at targets in Israel and at American bases in the region, in a response that, while significant, appeared calibrated to demonstrate the ability to retaliate without immediately escalating into all-out war. Israel and the United States, for their part, maintained “heavy and surgical” military operations, according to statements by American officials, insisting that the attacks would continue as long as they considered that Iranian military infrastructure represented a threat. In the following days, until the present, the international debate focuses on two issues; the first, who will succeed Khamenei and the second, how his death will reconfigure the balance of forces in the Middle East. It is worth remembering the leader's journey, from his participation in the 1979 Revolution, through his presidency during the Iran-Iraq war, to the decades in which he consolidated a strongly security theocratic system, supported by a vast network of militias and regional allies. At the same time, excerpts of Khamenei's recent speeches were circulating again, including a February speech in which he described the United States as an empire in decline and insisted on the need for Iran to maintain “deterrent weapons” capable of sinking American warships. In that same intervention, he said that his body was “worth little” and that, even if they killed him, the true loss would only occur if the Iranians abandoned the principles of the revolution. These words, reinterpreted in light of his death, were used both by supporters, who read them as a political testament, and by opponents, who saw them as the last echo of an era that came to an end under foreign fire. Domestically, the regime sought to control the narrative. Censorship intensified, internet access was restricted in several regions and security forces were mobilized to prevent large unauthorized gatherings. Still, videos of celebrations and small protests continued to emerge, signaling a society deeply divided between ideological loyalty, fear of chaos and desire for change. At the same time, rumors began to emerge about possible balances of power between conservative factions, Revolutionary Guards and high-ranking clerics, all aware that the choice of the next leader will define the country's course for decades. Abroad, governments and international organizations reacted with a mix of caution and concern. Some countries have highlighted the risk of regional escalation, given Iran's central role in conflicts in Syria, Lebanon, Iraq and Yemen. Others saw Khamenei's death as an opportunity to reopen diplomatic channels, hoping that a new leadership might be more open to compromises on the nuclear program and regional policy. At the same time, the possibility of a prolonged power vacuum, with infighting among Iranian elites, has been identified as a scenario that could increase instability, fuel refugee flows and create space for extremist actors. As for the future, the next few months are likely to be marked by three main dynamics. Firstly, a silent but intense dispute within the Iranian system, around succession, in which the Revolutionary Guards will seek to ensure that any new leader preserves their interests and autonomy, while clerical sectors may try to regain some religious primacy over the military apparatus. Secondly, Iranian society, especially the younger generations, will continue to test the limits of the regime, alternating between moments of mobilization and periods of withdrawal, depending on repression and political opportunities. Thirdly, the relationship with the outside world will be marked by a delicate balance between confrontation and negotiation, as Iran will be unlikely to abandon its stance of resistance towards the United States and Israel, but may, due to economic and political necessity, explore ways of partial easing with Europe, neighboring countries and powers such as China and Russia. It is also plausible that, in the short term, a high level of military tension will remain in the region. Militias allied with Iran, such as Hezbollah in Lebanon or Shiite groups in Iraq, may intensify low-intensity attacks to show that the “resistance network” remains active, even without Khamenei. Israel and the United States, for their part, will remain vigilant and willing to respond with force to any action they consider a direct threat. However, all actors know that an open and prolonged war would have incalculable costs, which could act as a brake on an uncontrolled escalation. In the longer term, Ali Khamenei's death will probably be remembered as a turning point and the end of a leadership that shaped the political identity of post-revolutionary Iran, and the opening of a period in which nothing is guaranteed, neither the rigid continuity of the current system nor a rapid transition to a more plural model. Between official mourning, celebrations in the streets and the tense silence in the corridors of power, the country finds itself, in these first days of March 2026, suspended between the past that closed under bombs and a future yet to be written. The evolution of the war between the United States and Israel against Iran, as well as the impact of Iranian attacks on several Arab countries, reveals a conflict that has expanded rapidly and caused profound disruptions in regional security, aviation and international trade. The United States and Israel have focused efforts on destroying Iranian military capacity, hitting command centers, Revolutionary Guards infrastructure and projects linked to missiles and naval operations. The American President admitted that the operation could last for weeks, justifying it with the need to eliminate threats, stop Iranian support for armed groups and prevent advances in the nuclear program. Israel, for its part, has intensified attacks on Tehran and strategic installations, ensuring that it will continue to target Iranian decision-making centers. In response, Iran launched missiles and drones against Israel, American bases and several Arab countries, claiming to act in self-defense and promising to punish any aggression on its territory. The expansion of the conflict became evident when Iranian attacks hit at least eight Arab countries such as Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Jordan, Iraq and Oman, causing damage to airports and civil infrastructure, especially in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Bahrain and Kuwait, where terminals were damaged and flights were cancelled. The closure of airspace in several of these countries led to thousands of cancellations and disruptions to international traffic. At the same time, Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, through which a significant part of the world's oil flows, increasing the risk of a sharp rise in crude prices and exposing global economic vulnerability. These attacks reveal the Iranian intention to increase the regional cost of the American and Israeli offensive, reaching not only military targets, but also strategic economic centers in the Gulf. The international community reacted with growing concern. The European Union, the UN and several governments have warned of the risk of a prolonged war with unpredictable consequences for the Middle East and the global economy. The UN Secretary-General called for an immediate ceasefire, stressing that the escalation threatens international stability. Arab countries, although critical of Iranian aggressiveness, fear being drawn into a conflict they do not control. Some, like Qatar, have openly condemned the escalation, while others have reinforced security measures and sought diplomatic channels to avoid direct involvement. As for the future, three main scenarios seem plausible. The most likely, in the short term, is a prolonged escalation, with Iran maintaining attacks to preserve credibility and deterrence, while the United States and Israel intensify operations to degrade Iranian military capacity. In this scenario, neighboring Arab countries will continue to be vulnerable to indirect attacks, even without actively participating in the war. A second, less explosive scenario, points to a controlled but unresolved conflict, marked by cycles of attacks and counterattacks, in which Iran could opt for calibrated responses to avoid total destruction, while the United States could reduce operations after declaring objectives achieved, while still maintaining military and economic pressure. A third scenario, less likely in the short term, involves sufficient diplomatic pressure to lead to a ceasefire, something that could occur if the global economic impact, especially on the oil market, becomes unsustainable, or if powers such as China, Russia and European countries intensify mediation efforts, or even if Iran faces internal difficulties that limit its ability to maintain the conflict. For neighboring Arab countries, the consequences are evident, such as internal instability fueled by fear of new attacks; severe economic impact on the aviation, tourism and energy sectors; increasing pressure to choose sides in a conflict they would prefer to avoid; and the risk of unintended military involvement if further attacks cause significant casualties or critical damage. The region thus finds itself in a fragile balance, where each new attack can change the course of the war and further expand its scope.

item.title: What democracy is this?
item.description: From today onwards, Portugal will have a new President of the Republic. António José Seguro, after having been away from politics for ten years, decided to run without anyone's political support and became the winner. Seguro will face a mandate in Belém with the most diverse difficulties as he has to deal with a country full of problems. Portuguese democracy is getting sick. What kind of democracy is this where a Government passively participates in a military attack on a sovereign country by administrations led by fascists, especially the American administration led by an individual who just wanted to divert attention from the Epstein case where he allegedly was one of the participants in the sexual abuse of minors. Portugal freely allows the Lajes Base to serve as one of the largest and busiest platforms for North American warplanes, which have participated in a war that is spreading throughout the Gulf. What a democracy it is where a leader of an extreme right-wing party, in the plenary of the Assembly of the Republic, begins to offend the acting president of Parliament, going so far as to say that the president shouldn't even be sitting there. The acting president's tough and cordial response received applause from all the benches, including the communists, because the people were really starting to get fed up with André Ventura's diatribes. What a democracy this is that clearly repudiates the patriotic attitude that the Spanish government had regarding the prohibition of the use of American bases on Spanish soil in participating in non-compliance with International Law. What a democracy it is in which the poor citizens who were the target of storms and who were left with nothing, continue to complain that financial support has not reached them and that the reconstruction of their homes continues at ground zero. What a democracy it is in which citizens notice every month that the cost of living increases, that the price of houses is becoming unbearable and that the price of fuel increases in a frightening way. What a democracy this is where the construction of a megalomaniac airport is planned when four million Portuguese people live at the level of poverty. What a democracy it is that continues to charge tolls on the 25 de Abril Bridge when it has been more than paid for for many years. What kind of democracy is this that governmentally confuses things with mistakes, that is, a director of the Judiciary Police, who for many years was on top of the most varied cases under judicial secrecy and who had access to the supposedly criminal activities of politicians, including many suspected of corruption and illegal activity as government officials, could never be invited to become Minister of Internal Administration. What a democracy it is that allows pensioners to exist with a monthly nest egg of 200 or 300 euros and then sends millions of euros to Ukraine. What a democracy it is that allows a President of the Republic to finish his term without concluding anything about what happened to his son in the discriminatory case of the Brazilian twins. What a democracy it is that a former prime minister has been waiting for more than ten years to be tried. What a democracy it is where immigrants, absolutely necessary for the national economy and for the support of Social Security, panic with Chega's demands from Montenegro for immigrants to return to their homeland. We imagine what would happen to Portugal if all countries where Portuguese emigrants live took the same measures. What a democracy it is where an investor who owns a large house near Sintra and has luxuriously remodeled it to be a charming hotel, has been waiting for almost two years for municipal licensing to open its doors. It should be noted that this investor always refused to pay anything outside the legal norms. Perhaps this is why licensing is approved for calendas. The new President of the Republic may be a sensible, moderate man, defender of the Constitution, a democrat, imbued with the best intentions, but the Constitution itself has never been fulfilled with regard to the obligation of every Portuguese person to have the right to a home to live with dignity. And it will not be António José Seguro who will enforce the Constitution because we are sure that at the end of his term there will be Portuguese people living in tents, others in their parents' homes and still others in their children's homes. And what can we say in this democracy where a politician appears who disgraced the most disadvantaged, who removed holiday and Christmas subsidies, who reduced reforms and who now appears, like D. Sebastião, wanting to take Luís Montenegro's place as leader of the PSD. Pedro Passos Coelho should turn black for the political activity he has been carrying out against the leadership of his own party. His intentions are fully known. He wants to be leader of the PSD again, he wants to be prime minister again to execute an alliance with Chega and implement a neo-fascist government. Poor democracy that has “democrats” of this kind, always ready to stick the knife in their partner…

item.title: “Three regions” events: from pioneering to institutionalization
item.description:
By Manuel Silvério – former president of the Sports Institute and co-founder of the Macau Olympic Committee
The written question from Deputy Leong Sun Iok raises a timely and, above all, concrete point: how to transform recent experiences — in particular the cycling event that linked Macau, Hong Kong and Guangdong — into institutionalized advantages, capable of reinforcing regional cooperation and consolidating Macau as a Sports City. The merit of this position also lies in the profile of those who place it. Leong Sun Iok is a young deputy, close to the field, who regularly accompanies athletes and practitioners. And it is not insignificant that it is linked to the Federation of Workers' Associations of Macau (FAOM), an institution with strong social and sporting traditions, where many young people — and also “anonymous” practitioners — have found, over decades, space to train, compete and create active lifestyle habits. Just remember the emblematic Campo dos Operários, in the heart of the city, next to the Portuguese School, where the Grand Lisboa stands today — a symbol of how Macau has changed, but where the sporting memory remains. The central issue is simple: pioneering experiences are important, but what counts is what happens afterwards. And this is where the deputy's question is decisive: when introducing or hosting international events in the future, will there be conditions to take advantage of the experience gained in the National Games? Will there be a mechanism to facilitate authorizations and coordination with Guangdong and Hong Kong?
What the Government might do “next” (realistic forecast) Without guessing too much, there are a set of steps that the Government could realistically take to transform these experiences into a permanent capacity:
⁠Create an interdepartmental working group (security, traffic, tourism, sport, border issues and health), with regular meetings and a clear decision-making method. Establish a “green channel” for “three-region” events, with defined criteria, fixed deadlines and a predictable administrative circuit. ⁠Consolidate an annual or multi-annual calendar with 1–2 anchor events (e.g. a cross-border cycling race and a half marathon/border crossing race). Strengthen cooperation with Guangdong and Hong Kong in a technical format, not just political: operational protocols, contingency plans and coordination between teams on the ground. ⁠Secure partners with operational capacity and sponsorship, including robust private sector participation, where this increases efficiency and scale.
The risk is known: everything will be left with “good intentions” if there is no owner of the process — a leading entity — and execution goals with accountability. If Macau really wants to gain scale and regional relevance through sport, the path passes less through announcements and more through mechanisms, deadlines, leadership and execution. Deputy Leong Sun Iok's interpellation, in this sense, is a good starting point to ask the right question — and, above all, to demand operational answers.

item.title: The Geopolitics of Flashpoint
item.description:
“Power is never silent; when diplomacy fails, it speaks in the language of escalation.” Robert D. Kaplan
The triangular relationship between the United States, Israel and the Islamic Republic of Iran has constituted, since the end of the 1970s, one of the structuring axes of international politics in the Middle East. Its evolution over almost half a century reveals a pattern of persistent antagonism, shaped by ideological, strategic, security and civilizational factors that reinforce each other. From 2020 onwards, and with greater intensity after 2022, this antagonism acquired a systemic dimension, as it began to intersect with broader global rivalries, namely the strategic competition between the United States, China and Russia and directly influence the stability of energy markets, the regional security architecture and the very credibility of international norms regarding nuclear proliferation. The genesis of this conflict dates back to the Iranian Revolution of 1979, which overthrew the regime of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, a central ally of Washington, and established a theocratic republic based on a Shiite revolutionary ideology. The hostage crisis at the US embassy in Tehran, which lasted 444 days, crystallized a perception of irreconcilable hostility between the two states. The hostage crisis left an indelible mark on mutual perception, cementing the image of the United States as the “Great Satan” in Iranian revolutionary doctrine. This rupture was not just diplomatic, as it represented a profound reconfiguration of the regional order, by replacing a pro-Western pillar with a regime that saw itself as the vanguard of an anti-imperialist and anti-Zionist revolution. For Israel, the rise of the Islamic Republic constituted an existential threat. Until then, the Shah's Iran functioned as a strategic counterweight to Arab nationalism and Iraq's regional ambitions. From 1979 onwards, Israel began to face a State that not only rejected its legitimacy, but was ideologically committed to its elimination. The emergence of an openly anti-Zionist Islamic Republic represented an existential threat, permanently altering Israeli security calculations. This perception became even more acute with Iranian support for non-state actors such as Hezbollah in Lebanon and, later, for Palestinian groups such as Hamas and Islamic Jihad. Iranian foreign policy, especially under hard-line leadership, is based on an ideological vision that considers the international order led by the United States to be illegitimate. This vision supports the construction of the so-called “Axis of Resistance”, a network of state and non-state actors that includes Hezbollah, Shiite militias in Iraq, armed groups in Syria, the Houthis in Yemen and Palestinian factions. The objective of this network is to project influence, create strategic depth and increase the costs of any military action against Iran. The underlying logic is clear because by dispersing the battlefield across multiple theaters, Tehran seeks to compensate for its conventional inferiority vis-à-vis the United States and Israel. On the Israeli side, the national security strategy is based on three pillars with maintaining a qualitative military advantage, preventing any regional rival from acquiring nuclear weapons and the ability to carry out preventive operations to neutralize emerging threats. This doctrine, developed since the 1960s, was reinforced after the 1973 war and consolidated with the preventive attack on the Iraqi nuclear reactor in Osirak, in 1981. From the 2000s, with the advancement of the Iranian nuclear program, Israel began to consider Iran as the main strategic threat, and the possibility of Tehran achieving latent nuclear capability became the ultimate red line. The United States, in turn, plays an ambivalent role. As a global power, Washington seeks to guarantee the stability of energy routes, contain nuclear proliferation and preserve its credibility as a guarantor of security for its regional allies. However, since the invasion of Iraq in 2003, American policy in the Middle East has been marked by internal tensions between interventionist and isolationist tendencies. The withdrawal from the nuclear agreement (JCPOA) in 2018, followed by years of economic sanctions and indirect confrontations, contributed to an environment of deep distrust between Washington and Tehran. Between 2020 and 2025, the region saw an intensification of the so-called “war in the gray zone”, characterized by cyber attacks, industrial sabotage, clandestine operations and clashes through proxies. This phase can be considered as a period in which the conflict remains mainly in the shadows, with both sides seeking to inflict costs without crossing the threshold of direct confrontation. This dynamic reflects a logic of mutual deterrence, as each actor seeks to avoid an open war, but also to avoid appearing weak before their adversaries and their internal audiences. As of 2024, several factors have contributed to increasing the risk of escalation. Firstly, the Iranian nuclear program has advanced significantly, approaching levels of enrichment and technical capacity that, according to Western assessments, could allow a rapid breakout. Second, the growing assertiveness of Hezbollah and Iraqi militias has increased pressure on Israel and American forces in the region. From mid-2025, signs of accelerated deterioration became evident. Israel has intensified clandestine operations against Iranian scientists and facilities; Iran responded with cyber attacks and strengthening Hezbollah's military capabilities. In the Gulf, incidents involving commercial ships and drones have increased tension between Tehran and Washington. Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, despite efforts to reach diplomatic relations with Iran, remained deeply concerned about Iranian expansionism and the possibility of a regional arms race. In this context, the end of February 2026 emerged as a turning point. The combination of advances in Iran's nuclear program, proxy attacks against American interests, and Israeli fears that the window for preemptive action was closing created an environment in which deterrence began to fail. Deterrence theory teaches that this failure occurs when the threat of punishment no longer outweighs the perceived benefit of aggressive action. In the Iranian case, the perception that obtaining a latent nuclear capability could guarantee the regime's survival became a powerful incentive to resist Western pressure.
(Continues)

item.title: Concert | Kiri T premieres live on Saturday at the Broadway Theater
item.description:
The Broadway Theater will be the first stage in Macau that Kiri T will perform on. The debut concert is scheduled for Saturday and the singer, one of the main voices of the new generation of Hong Kong artists, promises to create an intimate atmosphere that frees her from shyness.
One of the most interesting voices of the new generation of Hong Kong singers makes his debut on Saturday in Macau. Kiri T's first concert in the territory is scheduled for the Broadway Theater at 8 p.m. Yesterday there were still tickets for seats furthest from the stage, which can be purchased at the online box office for 680 patacas. The concert, titled “Hello, Introvert” highlights the atmosphere that the artist wants to create at the Broadway Theatre: a sanctuary and “intimate place of emotional refuge, where introverts can release, connect and heal in freedom through music”, describes the organization of the show by the Hong Kong artist. Since appearing on the music scene in the neighboring region, Kiri T (Kiri Tse Hiu Ying) has been gaining dedicated fans, despite the wandering way in which he jumps between musical genres with each record, moving from traditional cantopop to more intimate indie sounds, or more expansive emo rock, with soulful detours into R&B or forays into electronic music.
