"One of them has already received several nominations and even awards in Hong Kong, including best actress [Jennifer Yu]. This time, after almost ten years, we worked together again. I think we grew a lot and it was like working among friends. It was a great happiness to be able to work with someone I know well. The chemistry between them is also very good, because they are friends in real life." In the case of Natalie Hsu, she was nominated for the “Best Actress” award at the 43rd edition of the Hong Kong Film Awards, while Eliz Lao is a local actress, who even had a film cycle under her own name at Cinemateca Paixão. Eliz Lao made films in Hong Kong and China, having experience in television and theater, without forgetting her training in ballet. Regarding today's premiere, Tracy Choi highlights that “films with LGBT themes, or stories about female growth, are not very commercial”. "Whenever we tried to enter festivals, we were told that the film would not have great sales. Still, even if the film does not have great commercial success, we hope to reach more audiences, and make more people aware of the film", he highlighted. “Girlfriends” was shot and produced with public funds, and filmed in 2024. Tracy Choi and her team also sought investment in Taiwan and Hong Kong. After launching in Macau, the team is preparing for new debuts in the neighboring region and in Taiwan. Regarding the recent experience in Busan, South Korea, with “Girlfriends” as part of the “Vision Section” competition section, Tracy Choi brought back the best memories. "It was a great experience to premiere the film there. It was the first Macau film to be shown at the festival, we had a great night. The public also seemed to like it and we ended up being invited to go to other festivals, and that was important for the whole team." Break through barriers Tracy Choi's cinema reflects a little on Macau's imagination and women's experiences, but also explores emotional scenarios of feelings of belonging, memory and memories. "When it comes to women and stories from Macao, we are always represented by others, like films from Hong Kong, or films from China. We are often represented by other directors and there are not many voices from local directors, especially when it comes to women. So, as I am a woman, I really hope to focus on how women grow up here or in other places in Asia, on what women face. In my next projects I hope to continue to focus on the ways women face the world in different situations," she said. Tracy Choi makes no secret of the fact that “Girlfriends” has an openly LGBT theme, whereas, in her previous films, it wasn’t so much like that. "Maybe they were more about Macau or [the feeling] of sisterhood, something like that. But in 'Girlfriends' we mainly focused on an LGBT love story. I hope it's a little more commercial for an LGBT film, and the way we chose to tell the story is more commercial. I hope that the general public, and not just LGBT, can also feel love for LGBT people and identify with that kind of story." In the case of “Sisterhood”, her first feature film, there are references to the transition of Macau, in 1999, and two former masseuses, Sei and Ling, who spent good times together. However, years later, one of them realizes the other's big secret, in a friendship that takes on different forms. The difficulties experienced Despite the success she has experienced, Tracy Choi highlights that “it is very difficult to be a director in Macau because there are not many productions”. "But it's not just me, other filmmakers in Macau are also working hard to make things happen. We look for other ways [to support ourselves]: I teach and work as a producer to make other productions come to fruition. We hope, all together, to be able to gain more audience and attention, so that the next directors, and productions in Macau, have a slightly easier path", he added. The director is still harvesting the laurels of her fourth film, but is already working on the post-production of another feature film. “Last year I also filmed another production, in co-production with China, and we hope to finish before the summer and show the film at the end of this year.” Tracy Choi was born in 1988 and studied Film Production at Shih Hsin University, having grown up in the San Kiu neighborhood. “Sisterhood” was his presentation film that gained him some notoriety, particularly after receiving the Audience Choice Award at the Macau International Film Festival. He was only 28 years old at the time. About this moment, he recalled to Revista Macau: "After graduating in Taiwan, I returned to Macau to work. At that time, about ten years ago, there was no cinema in Macau. There were no local productions being filmed regularly. That didn't happen and we took advantage of the opportunity given to us. I challenged my friends to join the competition organized by the Cultural Center, obtain financing and film something that had an important meaning for us. It was also very important that my first feature film could premiere at the Cultural Center, because it was something that brought visibility to the local film industry”, he said.

item.title: Marcelo Loureiro, author and teacher: “The indigenato was a continuation of slavery”
item.description: He was recently in Macau to present the book “Colonial Citizenship: law, race and rights through the Portuguese empire”. Marcelo Loureiro's work explores the way in which, in the Portuguese empire, Portuguese citizenship was shaped according to skin color. Macau was no exception, although with different contours compared to Africa, argues the professor at the University of Leicester Image: Rui Cunha Foundation How did this investigation begin and how did the themes of colonialism and citizenship emerge? Everything was born from a personal and family experience. I grew up in Rio de Janeiro, in a family marked by the Portuguese diaspora on the one hand, and colonial silence on the other. My paternal grandmother, Portuguese, kept the documents that allowed me, at the age of thirteen, to claim Portuguese citizenship at the bottom of a closet. My other grandmother, mestizo, cafuza, had no papers, no stories to tell; From her to me, there was only silence. This division, between those who have the right to documents and a voice and those who are forced to be erased, was the starting point for questioning the legal construction of citizenship and nationality. When I arrived in Portugal to study Law in Coimbra, I began to realize that the law that recognized me as Portuguese was the same one that historically denied this recognition to millions of people based on race, 'civilization' and skin color. The book is, in part, an attempt to understand this legal structure of inclusion and exclusion, it is a denunciation of the injustice behind rights. It is a denunciation of citizenship as a method of exclusion. In the context of the Portuguese empire, when did the first notions of citizenship emerge and what criteria were used? The first formal notions of national belonging in Portugal appear in the Philippine Ordinances of 1603, which establish who should be considered 'natural' of the kingdom. It is interesting to note that the inclusion of the notion of nationality was only included in Portuguese law during the Iberian Union, during the Philippine dynasty, when Spain controlled Portugal through the Habsburgs. It was in this context that Spanish law was transposed to Portugal to separate Portuguese from Spanish. However, the practical construction of Portuguese citizenship begins much earlier, in opposition: through slavery. Since the 15th century, with papal bulls (such as Dum Diversas of 1452 and Romanus Pontifex of 1454), Portugal obtained canonical legitimacy to enslave Africans and conquer their territories. Portuguese citizenship was thus constructed, in opposition to the figure of the ‘other’ – the Moor, the black, the pagan, the ‘savage’, and finally, the ‘indigenous’. What were the differentiation criteria? The criteria were essentially civilizational and racial: being Christian and, progressively, being white or recognized as ‘civilized’ according to European standards. Conversion to Christianity functioned, at times, as a kind of 'spiritual whitening', but even converts rarely achieved full legal equality, which continued to be imposed for centuries. The law called indigenous to all those who existed outside the standard of civilization expected of citizens, which were the non-white populations of Angola, Mozambique, Guinea and Timor. Macau will always have occupied a “privileged position” in the context of this empire. Specifically, how? In terms of citizenship, Macau has never been subject to the Indigenous Statute, which was in force in Angola, Mozambique, Guinea and Timor. This exclusion was not accidental. The Indigenous Statute applied to populations considered 'uncivilized' according to European racial and cultural criteria, being, in practical terms, essentially black populations from continental Africa and Timor. In Macau, the Chinese population, often called ‘Macau Chins’, although not European, were treated differently. There were laws that regulated the rights and customs of the Macanese population, with a complete law coming into force in 1909 and only abolished in 1949, for example. The Portuguese presence in Macau has always been accompanied by several other factors, such as British influence through contact with Hong Kong, the progressive political presence of Beijing, Portuguese political instability and the questioning of the existence of the Portuguese empire itself internationally. This meant that Portugal was unable to impose in Macau a sovereignty as powerful as that imposed in Africa and Timor. Were there cases of discrimination against the Chinese and Macanese communities before the 20th century? Discrimination existed, although of a different nature than that imposed in Africa. We can even see in literature, in the works of Senna Fernandes, for example, how the existence of walls, sides and parts of Macau, sometimes even ideological, for different populations, from Chinese villages to Christian cities. The same division occurred in the world of rights, only in an indirect way, and not through the direct imposition of indigeneity and the status of second-class national. Segregation and difference occurred socially, with difficulty accessing certain careers, the lack of invitations to birthdays because they were not ‘white’. This discrimination did not take the systematic legal form of indigeneity, as stated. The Macanese occupied an intermediate position, often functioning as intermediaries between the Portuguese and Chinese. Their ‘Portugueseness’ was recognized in an ambiguous way: they were Portuguese, but of a different type. We can mention that skin color was decisive in not placing Macau under the Indigenous Statute. Yes, skin color was absolutely decisive. The Indigenous Statute was applied in Angola, Mozambique, Guinea and Timor, territories with populations that are mostly black or ‘non-white’ in the colonial sense. Portuguese law even created the term 'oceanic race' to identify Timorese during the imposition of indigenato so that they could bring this population racially closer to African populations. Macau, Portuguese India, Cape Verde, and, to a certain extent, São Tomé and Príncipe, were excluded from this classification. The official criteria established 'uses and customs' and 'civilization' as the parameters to identify these people, but in reality these categories only functioned as a substitute for race: they were race, but by another name. The populations of Portuguese India and Macau, although not European, have always been spared the application of openly racist laws, despite local practices and policies imposing, most often indirectly, restrictions on the full citizenship of these populations, as I have described. Taking into account the Indigenous Statute, what evolution can you trace in terms of the concept of Portuguese citizenship? The Indigenous Statute, consolidated in the early 1930s, represented the maximum legal codification of racial exclusion. Indigenato was a legal form of racial segregation that created two categories of being Portuguese, namely being a Portuguese citizen (white and assimilated) and being indigenous Portuguese (the majority black). The indigenous people were legally Portuguese, but they were not citizens; they did not live with access to rights, but to restrictions, they lived, as I call it in the book, in a status of ‘negatory nationality’. Indigenous people could not vote, could not move freely, did not have the right to lawyers, were subject to forced labor, paid special taxes based on race, and could not attend the same schools or hospitals as whites. The evolution of the concept of Portuguese citizenship in this context is paradoxical: formally, everyone was ‘Portuguese’, but materially, citizenship was reserved for those who demonstrated ‘civilization’ through skin color or by proving themselves to be sufficiently assimilated despite being black. Assimilation was the only legal path to leaving indigeneity, but it required proving mastery of the Portuguese language, abandonment of African ‘uses and customs’, stable employment, ‘good moral conduct’, and military service. Very few could. What are the most flagrant cases of denial of rights that you can point to? One of the most flagrant is the system of forced labor, which functioned as a continuation of slavery under another name. Indigenous people were forced to work on plantations, mines and public works, often far from their families, in inhumane conditions. Another case is spatial and social segregation: separate hospitals for whites and indigenous people, segregated neighborhoods, different schools. In Guinea, indigenous people were forced to wear necklaces with metal plates. In Mozambique they were forced to pay radio taxes if they had a device. In Timor they were required to pay taxes if they moved from one local authority to another, or when they divorced according to local customs. The archives also show cases of indigenous people who, despite having moved to Lisbon, still continued to be treated as indigenous people. All thanks to a 1955 law that imposed indigenato status on any indigenous person in any part of the Portuguese empire – which was previously only applied in indigenato colonies. This was the case of the so-called ‘Lisbon Requests’, a group of assimilation applications that I found in the Lisbon Ultramarine Archive, where indigenous people who, having emigrated to the metropolis, tried to obtain recognition as citizens. The Indigenous Statute would be abolished years later, but the effects seem to have remained. I absolutely agree. And I say more: indigenato was a continuation of slavery itself, a reflection that is still felt today by so many communities touched by the Portuguese empire. The Statute was abolished in 1961, but decades of legal, educational and economic exclusion are not erased by decree. African populations have been systematically denied access to education, property, mobility and political participation for generations. This explains, to a certain extent, the difference between the different parts of the Portuguese empire, where Brazil, Angola, Macau and India occupy completely different spectrums in relation to levels of protection and access to rights. When the Statute was abolished, a context of restriction and necessity was already consolidated where the black majority survived without formal education and without access to basic rights. The post-colonial effects are equally profound: indigenato taught generations of Africans that their culture, language and way of life were inferior, and that to formally be someone, to be recognized as fully human before the law, they had to become white, they had to resemble, as much as possible, the colonizer. But even this formal assimilation rarely translated into real protection: legal recognition did not guarantee effective access to rights. Assimilation marks not only a historical period, but a way of seeing, feeling and surviving colonial history. This violence left marks that persist in Portuguese-speaking post-colonial societies and that can only be resolved when there is a process of acceptance, truth and reconstruction — something that the Portuguese State has not yet been able to do.

item.title: Exhibition | Eric Fok shows “Dreaming of the Occident” in Taiwan: “In Portugal, I see traces of my city”
item.description: The year began with a new project by Eric Fok, who despite living in Portugal did not leave Macau and the East. “Dreaming of the Occident” is on display at the Helios gallery, in Taipei, and can be seen until January 24th. To HM, the local artist reveals that he wants to artistically explore the universe of Vendas Novas bifana, through its connection to Macau pork chop How is the West represented in this exhibition? I've always been fascinated by the intersection between Eastern and Western cultures, and I've explored how Macau was a territory shaped by Western missionaries and navigators who brought new sciences and ideas to the East. Regarding a previous exhibition, and during the research process, I realized how rare it was for the Chinese to travel to Europe at the time of the Discoveries. I read with enthusiasm the reports of the few who did so. There are diaries that vividly recreate the dangerous sea voyages and what life was like in ancient European cities. This allowed me to project myself into their stories, bridging the gap between past and present, between East and East. These cultural differences are not just history to me, but have become a rich source of imagination for my new work. As an artist from Macau, how has the history of the territory, marked by exchange between East and West, shaped your way of thinking about this exhibition? It all started with mapping Macau for the “Paraíso” series [2014 exhibition], a project that was born out of my curiosity about the city's past in a context of rapid expansion of the tourism and gaming industries. Through this dialogue with local history, I began to truly understand the city. I went from focusing on the small details of urban life to having a macro perspective of the world, highlighting Macau's significant role in global history. This includes its status as a gateway to China during the Discoveries, its contribution to the beginning of globalization, or even being a space for the spread of Catholicism to the East. [We cannot forget] the profound influence of maritime culture. All of these themes constitute an extension of my exploration of the historical and unique experience of Macau. Can you describe in more detail the creative process for “Dreaming of the Occident”? In addition to maps of Macau, this exhibition also presents maps of Eurasia and several Asian colonies, where I explore themes such as navigation, trade and empire. My research also extends to experimenting with materials. In addition to using paper and wood, I incorporated unconventional supports, such as ostrich eggs. These objects function as canvases to narrate stories about endemic species and the history of migration, establishing a bridge between a biological and historical side. Does the exhibition intend to criticize the Western influence in Macau, reflect on it or simply observe it? In my exploration of these themes I am mainly interested in the cultural dimensions of history. When Europeans first arrived in Asia, they viewed the world from a Eurocentric perspective. The term “Far East” was not just a geographical designation, but a symbol of power and a specific vision of the world. But, on the other hand, the term “Far West” represents an initial global imagination, in the sense of being a space of mutual understanding, albeit incomplete. Today, as we live in an era of a lot of information, where connectivity is instantaneous, we have lost that sense of mystery, and my work seeks to recover a little of what is the fantasy of distance. Do you expect different readings of the exhibition or a deeper knowledge of what Macau is, in cultural and historical terms? In the creative process, I privilege personal expression as responses to society and the world in which we live. Although exhibiting in a commercial gallery inevitably brings a market dimension to my work, my focus is always on the integrity of the work. In recent years, my desire for the public to “correctly decode” my art has changed, in the sense that, although I feel deep satisfaction when someone connects with my inner world, I have come to accept this “emptiness” in the narrative of my work. These gaps are meant to be filled by the experiences and emotions of the viewer, or perhaps by time and history itself. In a time of rapid and ephemeral changes, I use the brush to document and preserve. My work consists of sharing the urban experience that invites the public to create an emotional connection with cities, promoting a deep reflection on identity and belonging. He has lived between Macau and Portugal. How does this influence your work and your view of Macau’s history and culture? At the moment I respond to this interview I find myself in Vendas Novas, a town in Portugal that is famous for bifana. I am currently exploring whether there is any direct connection between this local specialty and Macau pork chop bread. Centuries ago, the Portuguese sought to build a “home” in Macau, leaving behind Portuguese structures that defined the skyline of my city. Currently, being in Portugal, I see traces of my city everywhere. Being Asian, and being surrounded by different races and languages, I find a certain sense of belonging that can be both alienating and deeply familiar. The enormous geographical distance is shortened by culture and history, which are shared. This exchange of identities and the movement between places constitute deep inspirations for my work. Searching for an unknown place On the Helios gallery's internet portal, Eric Fok's new exhibition is described as a search for a Europe that, on the eastern side, was known to exist, but which was far from being known, especially from the middle of the 16th century, when the term “Taixi” appeared as a reference to the “Great West”. The Chinese expression was used by the Jesuit missionary Matteo Ricci and is one of the focuses of the Macau artist's work in “Dreaming of the Occident”. Segundo a apresentação da Helios, “Taixi” era um termo atribuído à “terra extremamente distante no Ocidente e que foi, outrora, um espaço em branco nos mapas, ainda por explorar”. Desta forma, os mapas imaginados de Eric Fok exploram as ideias de um lugar ausente e desconhecido, sendo analisada também “a identidade do eu, o transporte de memórias, poder e ideologia”. Com recurso a pesquisa histórica, o artista de Macau “redescobre a ‘Taixi’ a partir do olhar do Extremo Oriente, por meio da escrita e da representação cartográfica”. Eric Fok investigou também “as visões do mundo que existiam no passado, formadas pela interligação entre o poder e a crença”, é descrito.

item.title: Piménio Ferreira: “Anti-gypsy rhetoric has always worked in Portugal”
item.description: Piménio Ferreira, better known as Gitelles Ferreira, anti-racist activist, 34 years old, physical engineer, Romani national. He is a man with a provocative speech, a corrosive, gentle humor and a smile that leaves no one indifferent. Gitelles, we live in an intercultural land and gypsies – in particular – have lived in Portugal for around five centuries. What happens so that gypsy communities continue to be “on the margins” of so-called society? We need to change the model of society. This is extremely stratified, placing people in subordinate positions to privilege a violent minority. The white social structure is patriarchal and racist. What is called the ‘margin’ of society is in fact an intrinsic place within it, which belongs to it. In other words, gypsy people have been included in this model of society since they discovered Europe and its kingdoms. The problem is the model of society that is maintained and the place to which they were relegated and how they were relegated: with violence, in a status of inferiority compared to white people, without the right to human dignity, to found a family, to choose their own language and religion, expropriated of any and all cultural and material rights. A place built and maintained to this day by genocidal and ethnocidal violence and persecution. In 1463, Vasco Gitano was granted an estate. In 1521, the gitanos, or gypsies, were already used as a reference for everything that is considered bad in the model of society. The “counter-example” compared to white that would stand out in a positive way. The ‘angel’ and the ‘demon’; the ‘other’, the enemy; the one against whom any attack and persecution is legitimate. Including the prohibition of defending oneself, having weapons, any type of property, and the right to stay. And is it because they are denied this right to stay that we still have nomadic gypsy communities within the country today? Yes, without a doubt. There are many ways to force nomadism: directly, by prohibiting permanence and expelling with police or military force; indirectly, refusing public or private housing to Roma people, because they are Roma; and also through the gentrification process. I read the results of a survey by the European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights, where it is said that approximately 80% of Roma respondents live below the risk of poverty threshold in their country; one in three gypsies lives in a house without running water; one in three Roma belongs to a family in which someone went to bed hungry at least once in the previous month; and 50% of Roma aged between 6 and 24 do not attend school. Why does this happen like this? It is the historic persecution that continues. There is no impoverishment that does not have an external cause. In this case, a political system of total expropriation is imposed on the basis of violence. As early as 1648, D João IV, the Restorer, prohibited ‘giving or renting houses to gypsies’. The same D João IV who achieved the kingdom of Portugal thanks to D. Jerónimo da Costa and his 250 Knights. The same D. Jerónimo da Costa who died defending Portugal's independence from Castile. Gypsy, like his wife and children. Among prohibitions on staying, “that gypsies do not enter the kingdom and those who are in it leave” (D João III, 1526), ​​with expulsions, banishment to the galleys, expropriation of goods and properties, imposed with penalties of flogging, mutilation and death, systematically carried out to this day (even in 2011 the Faro City Council wanted to ban gypsies in the municipality). The prohibition of working for others (no one employs gypsy workers, and those who do do so in complete precariousness, for poorly paid positions and without rights and dignified conditions). They are unable to remain dependent on self-employment, they are unable to invest, due to extreme expropriation. They become dependent on precariousness. If you want to know what it's like to live in pure neoliberalism, get to know the lives of gypsies. The reality of these is paradigmatic of the aggression and lack of protection that the “lords” are willing to go to, for their own benefit. What are you talking about, is it economic and political power? The group of political, social, cultural and economic power. The owner, the colonizer. This group has been in power since it instituted the current system of domination. And that only makes sense within the hierarchically stratified sociopolitical model in which we live. Hence the need to put an end to ‘lords’ and establish a new model of society, healthy, free, without hierarchies or strata, whether of race, class or gender. The model in which we live leads the gypsy community (and beyond) to economic exclusion, removing them from access to the consumption of products or excluding them from their production process; political exclusion associated with lack of access to information; to education and consequently the social exclusion seen in the inequality of access to quality community areas such as restaurants, libraries, theater, cinema, concerts, healthcare, etc. How does a gypsy child feel when they realize they are not part of the whole? Children who never see themselves represented, not in films, not in books, not in cartoons, not even in an advertisement eating cereal, for example? Is this a disaster for self-esteem, is it a priori condemnation? That's really the question. Gypsy people are not “excluded from society”, on the contrary, they are included in it. The problem is the existence of what is cynically called 'social exclusion', an Orwellian term, and people are under duress. Which concentration and extermination camp. In fact, the Nazi concentration camps are a more visual embodiment of what is called 'social exclusion' and, as here, they were not an 'excluded' part of Nazi society, but rather its fundamental and characterizing structure. It is a place of violence, where people are kept by violence, dedicated to their extermination, and designed so that few people can leave. And yet they resist. Just like in the Nazi concentration camps. And what you listed as an example of the ‘consequence’ of this ‘exclusion’, is in fact the means by which this exclusion also happens and is maintained. Gypsy people are outside the cinemas, either as consumers or producers (but they go to the cinema). They are outside of school, education and the Academy. Either as ‘users’ or as producers (but they go to school and are at the basis of the construction of knowledge and academic and scientific debates). Even in the attack on the gypsy child's self-confidence, we speak of a mechanism for maintaining this situation. But gypsy people respond very well: with gypsy pride. Another resistance, another aggression. Gypsy people are thus included in society. In a place that belongs to this modern model of society. And they resist and counter the power of domination and insist on being in the city, in the cinema, in the theater, in the Academy, in all spaces. With a lot of struggle and always resisting, but they are there. Social exclusion, more than a “place outside of society”, is part of its social structure. Do you mean to say there is collective resistance? At work, already in a context of absolute precariousness, during the pandemic, gypsy people rolled up their sleeves and once again showed proactivity and creativity in adversity. They learned by watching and doing. And here, it is again women who jump to the front line, show their face, learn to use digital tools with their daughters or sons, and launch themselves into the network. And so they explore online sales as well as app-based work (transport or couriers). In housing, in a pandemic context in which evictions were not stopped – as the law itself should and dictates – residents did not stop organizing and fighting against unfair evictions and demanding dignity in housing; residents associations allied in their action with other housing and anti-racist organizations. It is also worth noting how they reinvented themselves and intensified to collect and distribute food items and mask/alcohol gel kits to the most expropriated people. Gypsies and non-gypsies. In the anti-racist fight, new movements emerged and their actions intensified through social media. People who had never organized or become active before began their first steps in activism, joining this path of gypsy and anti-racist organization and popular action. People forget, or don't want to appreciate, but gypsy action has helped to determine results in the political dispute. Gypsy citizens mobilized in large numbers and determined electoral results in the 2017 local councils, both in Alentejo and in the Lisbon Metropolitan Area, even causing electoral earthquakes. And even in the Presidential elections, second place was decided by an amount equivalent to the estimated number of gypsy voters. Apart from the non-gypsy votes that were mobilized by the action of gypsy voters. Gypsy people are even co-building power in opinion spaces, in local media, on social networks and in party and institutional political disputes. And most importantly, in popular action and in alliances with other social movements. We never stopped fighting by all means and we continued to resist. On the other hand, there is more and more hate speech, from the far right in particular. What do you have to say about that? To begin with, these speeches are historical and European. They are neither current nor “extreme right-wing”. These speeches have been adopted and delivered for a long time, even in 'democracy and freedom', by various representatives of the center-right, conservatives, center-left, liberals, liberal left, even representatives of the PCP. Anti-gypsy rhetoric has always worked in Portugal, as it is the most white-natured rhetoric. That is, it allows the creation and recognition of “white” as the superior of all. And thus please the white electorate who, when they feel good, compensate with their vote. Even if the policies implemented by your elected officials harm you even more. Finally, I would like you to talk a little about your personal experience and how you became an activist? I studied, fought for my studies, made alliances, graduated, and entered the “formal precarious” job market. I started my militant action and here I am today. We can transform this society into a better one. This certainty, combined with the fact that the current one has so much suffering, makes anyone want to have a transformative attitude. I wanted to be an ‘inventor’ (physical engineer) and an anti-racist activist. And through study (not necessarily schooling) and organization with other people, in turn, with other studies, training and visions, I learned how the world works and how it can be changed. But before transforming, we need to survive. This gives rise to two types of actions: transformative and survival. The fight against hate speech appears as survival, but its defeat will be a transformative victory. There is a lot that – as people who want a better world – we have to do, namely work to criminalize hate speech; condemn and ban organizations with racist speeches and practices; denounce the racist institutional structure and logic that organizes Power and manages community resources. And in this sense, there have been various types of actions, from petitions to demonstrations. And we need more. In addition to being a member of the SOS RACISMO movement, I participate in the Podcast “Viemos Para Ficar”, with Mamadou Ba and Joseph da Silva. And if everything goes well, we will soon start a new project. With friendly people, we also have the “Cigana Initiative” project, dedicated to reflecting and promoting transformative movements. Essentially focused on educating against anti-gypsyism, denouncing historical and ongoing genocide and working towards a new societal project. Also with new projects in the pipeline. And finally, there are always voluntary actions, without an organization, where I try to share what I discovered about Anti-Gypsyism and Romani history in schools and with interested people. In addition, there is still the struggle for housing, because evictions did not stop during the pandemic. Due to housing and employment precariousness, racialized individuals, especially Roma nationals, are the most vulnerable to Covid-19. It is essential to have actions in this area; Of all the struggles, this is the most structural, as it goes against one of the main enemies of a healthy society: the real estate speculation of this capitalist system, which puts profit and goods above people and their humanization.

item.title: Kart: new INHAME competition is born in Macau
item.description: It is next weekend that the Coloane Kartdrome will host the first of seven rounds of the most recent championship integrated into the IAME international family of karting competitions, the IAME Series Macau After the MSAR established itself as a mandatory stop on the IAME Series Asia calendar and the Macau International Karting Grand Prix hosted the IAME Asia Final, the launch of the IAME Series Macau was confirmed, even before the New Lunar Year, comprising seven races to be held in Coloane. The calendar presented follows the already established AAMC Karting Championship. According to the brief communication released on social media by IAME Asia Series, “Macau’s motorsport heritage is profound and its passion for racing is recognized worldwide”. For the IAME promoter on the Asian continent, based in Singapore, taking the competition to the Macao SAR constitutes “another decisive step in the growth of karting in Asia-Pacific, creating more routes, more opportunities and a stronger community for young talents”. All the same The IAME Series are karting championships organized under the aegis of the Italian IAME (Italian American Motor Engineering), one of the world's largest manufacturers of engines for the sport. The IAME Series Asia calendar will also be expanded next year: in addition to the rounds in Malaysia and Thailand, Macau will host the fourth of six races of the season, scheduled for the 23rd and 24th of May. Regardless of country or region, all participants compete with IAME engines, under uniform technical regulations that ensure equal conditions on the track. The model prioritizes cost control and competition sustainability, placing emphasis on individual talent and sporting equity. In addition to Macau and the Asian championship, IAME already promotes competitions in Mainland China, Japan, Malaysia, Thailand, Sri Lanka, India and the Philippines. In this context, the organization believes that “together, we continue to build an interconnected platform across the region”. As previously announced, the IAME Asia Final, covering all categories, will return for the third consecutive year to the Macau International Karting Grand Prix program. This year's edition is scheduled to take place between December 11th and 14th. As previously announced, the IAME Asia Final, for all categories, will return for the third consecutive year to the Macau International Karting Grand Prix program. The date has already been set, with the event taking place between the 11th and 14th of December. 2026 exam calendar:* Round 1 – March 1 Round 2 – March 22 Round 3 – April 26th Round 4 – May 17th Round 5 – June 28th Round 6 – 6 September Round 7 – September 27 *all races will be held at the Coloane Kartódromo

item.title: Japanese Toshikazu Yamanishi breaks world record for walking half marathon
item.description: Japanese Toshikazu Yamanishi today set a new world record for the half marathon walking, completing the race, which took place in Kobe, with a time of 01:20.30 hours. Yamanishi's mark achieved today is the first to surpass the inaugural world record of 01:21.30 hours, approved by the World Athletics Council in December following the announcement that the half marathon and marathon would become the official road distances for walking events. Thus, since January 1st of this year, the distances of 20 kilometers and 35 kilometers in walking have been replaced by the half marathon (21.1 kilometers) and marathon (42.2 kilometers) Toshikazu Yamanishi, 30 years old, broke away from the lead group a little after 17 kilometers and ended up passing 20 kilometers with a time of 01:16.26 hours, just 16 seconds more than the world record for this distance that he himself set in 2025 on the same circuit. With another lap added, the two-time world champion accelerated again to win by a clear margin, in 01:20.34, with the time that constitutes the new record now awaiting the usual ratification process.

item.title: GP | FIA confident in the return of the F4 World Cup to Guia
item.description: The International Automobile Federation (FIA) expressed confidence in the integration of the second edition of the FIA ​​Formula 4 World Cup into the program of the 73rd edition of the Macau Grand Prix, and is currently in negotiations with the Organizing Committee with a view to achieving this objective At the end of last year, the FIA World Motor Sport Council confirmed the ninth edition of the FIA GT World Cup as part of the Macau Grand Prix program, as well as the permanence of the MSAR sporting event on the 2026 season calendar of the Kumho TCR World Tour, having, however, not commented on the future of the Formula Regional (FR) and Formula 4 World Cups. of the FIA Formula 4 World Championship was a success in the eyes of the international organization, and the only global competition at the top level of the motorsport pyramid is expected to return to the Guia Circuit between the 19th and 22nd of November. “The FIA ​​and the Organizing Committee of the Macau Grand Prix are in positive discussions regarding the future of the FIA ​​Formula 4 World Cup”, declared the FIA ​​spokesperson to the Portuguese specialized portal SportMotores.com. According to the same source, "the FIA ​​was very pleased with the 2025 edition in Macau and is committed to providing young drivers with the best possible preparation to face the remarkable challenge of the Macau Grand Prix. We believe that the FIA ​​Formula 4 World Cup provides the ideal platform to achieve this goal." The entry list for the first edition of the Formula 4 World Cup revealed a range of high-level drivers, exceeding initial expectations, as no less than eight Formula 4 champions accepted the challenge. For logistical and operational reasons, the FIA ​​limited the starting grid to twenty competitors. In what ways One of the aspects that the FIA ​​should clarify at the next World Council meeting concerns the maintenance of the model adopted in 2025, in which the Chinese company Mintimes, promoter of the Chinese F4 Championship, assumed the role of “Sole Operator” of the race, in conjunction with the Fédération Française du Sport Automobile (FFSA), responsible for technical support for the World Cup. All competitors used Mygale M21-F4 single-seaters, renamed Ligier JS F422, from the Chinese championship. These formulas, equipped with Renault 1.3 liter turbo engines, were rented to teams from Mainland China and operated by a single technical structure, the same one that organizes the French F4 Championship. As this is a relatively unusual concept, several drivers were accompanied at the Macau Grand Prix by members of their own teams, with the aim of supervising the work carried out by the French technicians. Roussel went down in history Jules Roussel became the first winner of a Formula 4 World Cup, after one of the most exciting races on the 2025 edition program, starring in a lively duel with compatriot Rayan Caretti. Despite the discipline shown during much of the contest, both ended up getting involved in a collision that took Caretti out of the race and decided it. Macau was represented by two drivers, with Cheong Man Hei being the best classified, finishing in 9th position. The former Chinese Formula 4 champion, Tiago Rodrigues, finished in 11th place, a result that did not reflect his competitiveness, as he compromised a better classification in the decisive race on Sunday due to an accident in the qualifying race on Saturday. No Portuguese-speaking country was represented in the race, as Brazilian Ethan Nobels was injured the weekend before the Grand Prix, and did not even travel to Asia. There is a possibility that Portugal will have a driver on the starting grid this year, given that Noah Monteiro is a serious candidate for the Spanish Formula 4 Championship title. In addition, he is the son of Tiago Monteiro, the first Portuguese driver to win the Guia Race and a recognized Grand Prix enthusiast, also benefiting from the sponsorship of KCMG, a structure led by Hong Kong businessman Paul Ip.

item.title: Superbikes | Miguel Oliveira from last to seventh in the second race in Australia
item.description: Portuguese rider Miguel Oliveira (BMW) finished the second race of this opening weekend of the Superbike World Championship in seventh place, after starting from 21st and last place and from eighth place on Saturday. In a race held under rain, Miguel Oliveira finished 32.135 seconds behind the winner, the Italian Niccolo Bulega (Ducati), who beat his compatriot Axel Bassani (Bimota) by 11.336 seconds, with the Spaniard Alvaro Bautista (Ducati) in third, at 17.790. The Portuguese driver started at the back of the field after having mechanical problems in the last laps of the superpole race, held three hours earlier, which relegated him from the ninth place he occupied on the penultimate lap to 18th place. The second race was marked by rain, which fell heavily and caused six falls. The driver from Cascais, who usually does well on wet tracks, was already 10th in the middle of the race. Miguel Oliveira, who made his debut this weekend in the Superbike World Championship, after 14 years in MotoGP, still recovered to seventh place on the last lap, after overtaking Spaniard Iker Lecuona (Ducati) just a few meters from crossing the finish line. Italian Nicollo Bulega, favorite to start this 12-round championship, completed the perfect weekend, adding victory in the main race this Sunday, the 23rd of his career, to victories in the superpole and in the first race, on Saturday. It was the Italian driver's sixth consecutive victory in Australia. The 31-year-old Portuguese driver is eighth in the World Championship after this first of the 12 scheduled rounds, 45 behind the commander, Bulega, who has the maximum score (62) after this first round. The next race of the championship will be in Portugal, at the Autódromo Internacional do Algarve (AIA), in Portimão, on March 29th.

item.title: GP | Qualification races return to Zhuhai
item.description: Before the Lunar New Year, the General Automobile Association of Macau-China (AAMC) announced the calendar for the next season of its speed competitions, Macau Roadsport Challenge and GT4, which once again serve as the qualification process for the support races of the 73rd Macau Grand Prix. As in previous seasons, the mini-championship will be played over two double rounds. The Zhuhai International Circuit thus returns to the program, hosting these races for the first time since 2018. It will also be the absolute debut, on this route, of the models used in the Macau Roadsport Challenge, Toyota GR86 (ZN8) and Subaru BRZ (ZD8), which will compete for the first time outside the usual competitive context. The 2026 season also features a relevant novelty: the holding of a non-championship test race at the Zhuzhou International Circuit. The permanent route in Hunan province, which last year hosted the qualifying races organized by the AAMC, will be the stage for an optional preparation race. It should be remembered that, in 2025, the association promoted a non-championship competition after the qualification was defined, then at the Lake Ruyi City International Circuit, in Pingtan. The two scoring days will take place in the neighboring city of Zhuhai, both in May. The first is scheduled for the weekend of May 8th to 10th, while the second will take place between May 28th and 31st, 2026. The Macau Roadsport Challenge races will determine the qualifiers for the Macau Grand Prix of the same name, while the competitions reserved for GT4 vehicles will define the participants of the GT Cup – Great Bay Race. The competition will maintain an exclusive tire supplier, continuing to rely on Pirelli, which will ensure direct and centralized sales of the compounds. During the test race, the use of tires purchased in 2025 will be permitted, but in the qualifying races it will be mandatory to use new tires purchased in 2026. Subscriptions with significant increase The registration fee increased significantly, from 15,000 to 55,000 yuan. This value now includes not only the two qualifying races, but also the test race, representing a substantial increase compared to previous seasons. In return, the organization ensures the unified transport of participating vehicles and eight tire/rim sets between the Guangdong International Circuit, where a significant part of the Macau teams are based, and the Zhuzhou International Circuit, in both directions. During the preparation event, each registered competitor will also be entitled to three nights' accommodation in Zhuzhou, in a three or four star hotel. Additionally, each competitor will benefit from support for official Pirelli tires, corresponding to an approximate value of 10,000 yuan.

item.title: Lamborghini | Asian debut of the Temerario GT3 could be in Macau
item.description: Lamborghini said goodbye to the Huracán GT3 at the 2025 Macau Grand Prix and is now aiming for the Asian debut of the recently launched Temerario GT3, scheduled for November, on the streets of our city The ninth edition of the FIA ​​GT World Cup, scheduled for the 19th to 22nd of November at the Guia Circuit, appears as the likely stage for the debut on the Asian continent of the latest competition car from the Sant’Agata Bolognese brand, in a season in which the new twin-turbo V8 engine model will mainly be in a phase of competitive consolidation in Europe and the United States of America. Due to the limitations inherent in the production and delivery of new cars, Lamborghini Squadra Corse never seriously considered the introduction of the Temerario GT3 in the Asia-Pacific market in its launch year, despite the interest expressed by several teams, including JLOC, the formation of the Japanese Super GT championship that local driver André Couto represented. Even so, there is a clear desire on the part of Automobili Lamborghini's competition department to put its new GT3 on the track in Asia this year, precisely in the context of the Macau Grand Prix. “I can confirm that no Temerario will be entered in the GT World Challenge Asia or other regional championships,” explained a spokesperson for the Italian brand to the Portuguese portal SportMotores.com. "However, we are working on the FIA ​​GT World Cup in Macau, a race of enormous importance for us, where we intend to be present in the best possible way. At this stage, we cannot provide any further details." The possibility of Lamborghini competing this year in the Macao SAR race with the old Huracán GT3 is ruled out. The model faced difficulties in 2024 related to the introduction of the mandatory and controversial torque sensors. Edoardo Mortara had a particularly ungrateful weekend, seeing his third best time in Super Pole canceled for exceeding the maximum power limit, later ending up abandoning the main race when his car shut down. Production limitations Unlike other competition departments of major manufacturers, the Lamborghini Squadra Corse is a relatively small structure. The main limiting factor in the implementation of the Temerario GT3 this season, especially outside Europe, is related not only to the availability of units, but above all to the priority given to adequate technical support from the factory. The United States of America, where the car will make its competition debut at the 12 Hours of Sebring in March, continues to assume a strategic role for Lamborghini, hence the brand's full-time presence in the IMSA SportsCar Championship through the Pfaff Motorsports team. For the current season, Lamborghini Squadra Corse has allocated seven examples of the Temerario GT3 to competition in Europe, divided between the GT World Challenge Europe and the DTM. The Italian manufacturer plans to field three cars in the GT World Challenge Europe and four in the DTM. Mercedes-AMG evaluates return The absence of Mercedes-AMG in last year's edition was one of the most talked about topics in the paddock. This decision was justified by the lack of opportunities for Asian client teams to test the complex torque sensors, at a stage in which the Stuttgart brand is going through a demanding process of transitioning its sporting activities from HWA to the new subsidiary Affalterbach Racing. “At this stage, it is not possible to say whether a Mercedes-AMG GT3 will compete in the FIA ​​GT World Cup in Macau in 2026,” an official source from the German manufacturer, who maintains high interest in the MSAR event, told Hoje Macau. "The race is extremely appealing, both from a sporting and strategic point of view. We will carefully analyze the conditions at the appropriate time and evaluate them together with the interested teams." It should also be noted that the Macau Grand Prix could symbolize the definitive goodbye of the current Mercedes-AMG GT3, as the star brand is already developing the next generation of its car for the category, whose debut in competition could take place in 2027.
